Project Liftoff December Finals Event Report

Heading into the December Finals, Team Liftoff decided to make no major changes to Project Liftoff after the November event. The November event was our best event of the year, and though our bot was not translating quite as well as we would have liked, it was hard to argue with the success.

In November, we noted the excellent translation of Flip N' Cut, our meltybrain control bot. While I suspect there are a few additional reasons why Flip N' Cut translated so well, one difference that we definitely think contributed was the use of lower kv motors. Flip N' Cut used 1000 kv Propdrive 2836 motors, while Project Liftoff typically uses the 1200kv variant. 

We left the November project liftoff as it was when we left the arena in November. The bot was functional, if imperfect, and most of the build time we had available was being spent on our 30lb robot, Plyohazard. We built a new Project Liftoff from the ground up, using the electronics and the hubmotors from Flip N' Cut. This was the robot that we would primarily use in December, with the robot from November as a spare.

One other small adjustment was a slightly modified titanium wheel design. We added large radii to the internal corners of the spikes, which we hoped would improve the longevity of our cleat wheels. This didn't end up helping wear much but decreased the occurrences of complete tooth shear.

For the minibot, we redesigned the chassis to use lighter drive motors, swapping from the Endbots 20mm lightning to the repeat robotics 16mm brushed drive motors. We also swapped to a spring steel wedge, which would allow us to replace the wedge more often. This means that the minibot could enter the cage with a fresh wedge for each match, improving it's ability to play the ground game. 

While preparing for December, we didn't pay too  much attention to the bracket. Project Liftoff has a limited ability to cater to each opponent. We can change blade length, direction of rotation, and spin speed, not much else. We were hoping to win our fist fight, but were not optimistic for the second fight against Sepio1, as Liftoff has struggled against midcutters in the past.

In the Komodo fight, we planned to hit the rear as much as possible. Getting a wheel seemed unlikely, and going weapon on weapon was a bad idea because his weapon has so much reach. Our weapon was at the optimal height to hit at the pulley, so if Komodo was ever spun down we would go head to head.

As soon as the fight started, our minibot got wrecked by Komodo. Spring steel was a bad idea. Other than that, the fight went as planned, and I was able to hit the sides and back consistently. Once we got a wheel, the fight was won.

The Sepio1 fight started with a lucky hit. We got the perfect bite to steal a wheel immediately, and then I just hung back, trying to time hits to the back of the bot and stay away from the weapon.

Chubby Unicorn was a mess. Things started well, but thanks to inertia a component came loose on one of our ESCs, setting it on fire. After that, I just focused on keeping the bot at speed and in the middle of the arena, and luckily Chubby Unicorn was already damaged enough for us to slide into a win.

We replaced the ESC and wheels, then buttoned up for the eruption fight. We new the minibot would be critical, and it came in clutch. After a bunch of roof shots and trades, we managed to get a few lucky hits on the back or Eruption, locking up one of the drive sides. A few pins and lucky hits later, and it went to a confident judges decision. This match showcased Liftoff's spinup and ability to recover from ricochets.

Lynx fight was as expected until it wasn't. We got one or two excellent hits on the back of the bot, but a wire came loose on the BEC and shut the robot off. Notably, Calvin went after the minibot hard from the start. Maybe he watched the Eruption fight, but he knew that minibot would be key to any victory we had a chance at.

Polywog fight was a winning fight, and then the bot went berserk. A receiver issue locked the bot into a spin and translate mode, and shutting down the transmitter to try to recover it shut the bot down completely. We got counted out with seconds on the clock. Brutal because it was totally winnable.

Yall's run was very impressive! PL is the spearhead of modern meltybrains, fighting the good fight. I still wish we coulda fought you in the quarterfinals, we appreciated you getting revenge on chubby unicorn for us :P

The electrical issues were unfortunate, the TX issue especially is nuts. How many matches did you run that receiver in? Alfredo has had it's fair share of shock related electrical issues, I think even potting in epoxy is not enough to prevent eventual solder hairline fractures under the conditions melties face. That's why we like our all-in-one boards and replace the electronics every event or so.

The first board (up to the lynx fight) was the one from flipncut (5-2 in November, plus 5 fights)

The second board (Polywog fight) was the old Project Liftoff board (6-2 in November)

Here is a bit of history...
Rev. 1 of Project LiftOff's electronics from March-2019 NHRL:

I think we took 5V power from the RC-Receiver

Next year I plan to build an ant-weight Melty using an Arduino Nano, a similar but better Accelerometer, and share the design and some starter code as a starter Melty kit.
The body, including the weapon, will be 100% TPU.  The motors, ECSs, and wheels (foam) will be off-the shelf components.

how much does the "multiple source of truths" affect noise? a student in my workshop has been using your resources and is investing time into cleaning their signals with the multiple accelerometer approach you've posted about. Also how beneficial is incorporating magnetometers or micro tachometers to the drive system? is it supplementary to the main accelerometers' readings? thanks for sharing all this valuable information - their project has been making meaningful progress 👍

<@185202043382661120> Great questions... The value of information depends upon its quality, age, and your ability to make use of it. I can write long responses to each of those questions because the answers depends upon the specific scenario and objectives.  Here are some general answers:
(a) accelerometers (and all sensors) have a noise component. The easiest way to cancel out the noise is to average the values, this is a form of filter, and there are more complex filters that can be used. But almost all filters "spread" the value in time. By using redundant sensors, we can average their values and smooth the noise, and avoid spreading our reading across time.  Ask your student(s) why spreading the accelerometer value across time matters...
(a-2) The need to filter a sensor value depends very much on the sensor, environment, and how well you use it. There may be an accelerometer out there somewhere that does not require post-filtering. Some accelerometers perform on-board filtering (like the H3LIS331DL), and that might suffice. There may be a different way of using my part.  Since I moved to that "dual accelerometers rotated 45 degrees" approach I have learned more, and might be able to get away with just one accelerometer... but to (re)build and (re)test... that is a bit of work, so I'll live with what I know works.
(b) magnetometers or micro tachometers to the drive system... that is "next level" melty stuff!  With more information, you can do more things. Knowing how fast the motors/wheels are spinning enables some exciting things for a melty.  Ask your students what they plan to do with that information...
(c) In 2020, we built a melty using wheel sensors instead of accelerometers. It was functional, and it could translate/drift, but it was not a good melty.
As indicated by (b), it can add some excellent supplemental information to improve the melty performance, but I would not rely on it to provide the rotational speed needed for a good melty.

gotcha, thanks! There's a lot more conceptuals to this style of signal processing than I thought haha. What was the name of the wheel sensor melty you made? Was it sensored brushless, from the hall effect, or some other outboard sensing effect?

It was Project LiftOff (same name), early part of 2020. It used the telemetry data from the BLHeli_32 ESC firmware (which returns electronic-RPS; divide that by the number of motor poles and you get the motor's RPS).  Some ESCs offer a third signal wire to return telemetry data. Most ESCs, not just BLHeli_32, support telemetry data.  The bidirectional dShot protocol returns the motors eRPS value at a very high data rate on the same wire that the dShot speed command is sent. dShot and bidirectional dShot are similar but different protocols... both are more advanced programming, but since I wrote my version, I think some dShot libraries for Arduino may have been created (I do not know, but would assume they're out there).

If you haven't seen it, here is Project LiftOff's history... https://wiki.nhrl.io/wiki/index.php/Project_LiftOff
It's due for an update... After December I will update it.

whats the benefit in having the accelerometers rotated in rev 6/7?? amazing to see the progress btw

you can use two of the axes to measure the centrifugal acceleration, and both axes will read 1/√2 of the total acceleration, so you can effectively get 41% more resolution out of the sensor. In other words, turning a 400g accelerometer 45 degrees will allow you to measure accelerations of up to 565g
And you can use 2 axes to get more than one reading of the force.
Imagine mounting the pcb at an angle too so you can get all 3 axes to show info

So `value A * 1/√2 + value B * 1/√2` and as A & B are pointed opposite to each other any noise ought to cancel out. e.g. noise adding to A subracts from B

I think

If you just rotate it at a 45 degree angle then yeah

Though I think the error is independent and it just ends up being RSS error reduction

Which, granted, is still basically free error reduction

well yeah, you get both systematic and independant error reduction

you're kinda averaging the independent noise and cancelling the systematic noise

I worked on something similar using ~9 IMUs for position tracking in denied GPS environments

Das a lotta IMUs

Makes sense for the systematic noise though
it did work really well

The final reason for 4 accelerometer readings at 45 degrees is that you can then get a set of equations that solves for the center of rotation! This eliminates the biggest issue with accelerometer melty!
Does it actually reduce error this much? The error being +-0.004 rpm is very impressive

If the accelerometers are actually at a perfect 180 degrees and there was no senor error, yes. In reality, a couple degrees of mounting error will introduce a single digit rpm error

It's wayyyy better than single accelerometer though. You can take damage mid match and it will adapt to the changing center of rotation.

Some of the "big updates" for Project LiftOff have been completed... I am now working on #4, and am thinking about skipping #5.

1) Traction Control ~~Anti-Slip acceleration algorithm~~
2) Drifting/translation as the Bot spins up
3) Improved IR sensors that can "see" 10 feet (instead of the 1 to 4 feet previously).
4) More "intelligent" and aggressive autonomous capabilities.
5) Battery Lids (no longer need to remove the entire top to swap batteries, 2-bolts instead of 16-bolts).

The new IR sensors are better researched parts, and better tuning of the circuitry that uses those parts.
I created a test setup and I did quite a bit of testing to maximize the sensitivity and range.  The funny thing is the first set of parts I bought off amazon, along with the original circuitry, had about 75% of the capability.  The second set of parts off amazon were not the promised phototransistor, but a photodiode, and my resulting range was about one foot.  Due to that catastrophic 1 foot range, I decided to fully test and verify the "improved IR sensors".

Yes.  Use to take 23 hours for each shell to print. Now it is down to 16 hours.  The seven hour difference is due to two things: (1) less material (to reduce weight), but I've only dropped about 30grams. (2) I reduced the number of "features", the newer/faster shells are just simpler shapes.

Check this out...

That is what my 4'x4' test cage looks like in IR when the Bot is near the center.
There are two sets of IR data being shown (blue and orange). The Blue is from the front sensor and the orange is from the back sensor.
~~I could not believe how identical they were!  The two data points on top of each other were taken one half rotation apart.~~

There are 512 data points taken during each rotation.

As you can see, there is a consistent effect in the corners that I assume has to do with how IR reflects off the corner walls.
You can also see the behavior when the sensor is looking straight at the wall.
Also, there are those "anomalous" isolated data points that each sensor saw... maybe a nail or paint or some dent in the wood.
I have not determined if the slight rotation is the data-pipeline-delay, or if the Bot was pointing slightly to the left.

The earlier versions of PLO were CNC'd out of HDPE or UHMW.  That process took between two and three hours (I tend to make my machines work on the slower side of their capabilities).  The weight is a bit lighter than TPU for the same volume. However, I think the TPU is more durable.  Also, I can print things that the vertical only one-sided CNC operation cannot generate.

I don't think it's the sensor speed. It's the reflectivity and angle of incidence of the IR signal on the wall. The last image right near the corner demonstrates this well,  because it increases as the angle gets closer to perpendicular to the wall,  then decreases as the angle moves from perpendicular,  then increases again as the corner acts as a literal "corner retroreflector"

Onto another topic: Traction Control
(1) Primary objective is to make each wheels' speed match the Bot's rotational floor speed, at the location of each wheel, as measured by the accelerometers. (At least for the steady state rotation, yet also when drifting).  The goal is to maximize traction by eliminating wheel slip.
(2) To be aware of: 
  (a) The eRPS motor telemetry is noisy, yet generally accurate (or else the ESC would not function).
  (b) if the CoM is off of center by 1mm in a certain direction aligned with the wheels, then one wheel path diameter will be +2mm and the other will be -2mm,  This means that the wheels must be driven at different speeds.
  (c) Motor KVs vary, even from the same manufacturing batch of the same rated motor.  This means that even if the CoM was dead center, then the wheels must be driven at different speeds.
  (d) At high Gs, expect some flex and some change in the wheel path diameters.
  (e) The accelerometer sensors have sizeable nonlinearities and +/- errors in their readings (and I am not talking about drift).

Short answer to your question, as Andrew said, is you should not have any problems.... but I have some concerns.
The longer answer depends on several things...
(1) Regenerative braking is required because it routes the energy into the battery or the other motor (when the motors share a common connection to the battery and if you accelerate the other wheel while you brake). Without regenerative braking,  the ESC and motor will get much hotter (the braking energy must go somewhere, and if not into the battery then into heat).
(2) IMO, for optimal melty drifting, you want to de-power/decelerate (brake) one wheel while you accelerate the other wheel (let's call that "motor pulsing").  The "motor pulsing" occurs for very short durations corresponding to each half rotation of the Bot, with "pulse windows" between 10ms (for a Melty spinning 3000rpm) to 30ms (for a melty spinning 1000rpm); these are short pulses, but as you indicated, they will occur constantly while you translate. Continuously pulsing motors will generate some heat; adequate air flow around the motors and ESCs can provide enough cooling to avoid overheating, but too little cooling will result in heat build up.  When we failed to allow adequate airflow, we experienced ESCs de-soldering themselves from the motors (and some ESC failures).

(3) Do **not ** "Brake on Stop".  That setting will generate large voltage spikes, and heat up your components. Just let the melty coast to a stop.
(4) Voltage spikes can be a problem by frying your electronics. When ESCs and motors are properly paired, ESCs are designed to handle the types of voltage spikes that ESCs and motors generate; if not properly paired, the ESCs can be damaged.  Capacitors help a great deal; most good ESCs have plenty of on-board capacitance; cheaper ESCs skimp on this and require you to add additional capacitance.  Project LiftOff's design uses two high discharge 4S batteries in parallel which provides additional power buffering. In addition, you should either use a BEC with a huge voltage range, or have your control electronics isolated from the ESCs' voltage spikes.
(5) Motor stalling is what tends to burn motors and ESCs. You are using higher KV motors than we run (we use 900kv to 1200kv).  You did not indicate if there is a gear ratio in play.  Starting a 2300kv motor when pinned could be an issue. If your wheels allow slippage, then that may avoid motor burnout (but at the expense of your spin-up time and ability to translate).  I'd add some logic to your melty software to avoid prolonged "high motor powers without melty movement".  Also, there are some ESC settings to help with stalled motors; set stall protections to max (this does come with some minor motor acceleration costs, but if you test the performance differences I doubt you'll find it worth worrying about).

There are a bunch of details from AM32 notes and conversations... I will paste them below

AM32: 
Running Brake Level : 
The force used to brake the motor when it is still spinning. Useful to reduce this if using large propellers and you do not want to stress the motor. Useful in rock crawlers to avoid performing endos. 
From my discussion with Pete: 
(a) For translational drift, we want a higher value. Only active when “Complementary PWM”=on.
(b) The fastest way to slow down without brake on stop would be to send 1 percent throttle to the ESC (and a higher value for "Running Brake Level".

Just to make sure I'm reading this correctly, by correctly tuning your running brake level and sending 1% throttle to the ESC, you can achieve a sustainable motor coast? Also, I know about the AM32 config differences, but are they still able to mimic the BLHelis regenerative breaking ability?

not quite...   AM32 offers a "Brake on Stop", if that =off, then sending power=0% to the motor will cause the motor to coast until motor/wheel/Bot friction causes it to stop. If Brake on stop = on, then setting motor power=0% (stop) will actively cause the bot to use power to make it stop FAST.
In AM32, "Running Brake Level" allows you to set how aggressively the motor will work to get to the (new) lower speed. A value of zero means it will coast to that speed; a value of 10 will apply the most power to change to that speed.
In AM32, "Running Brake Level" requires Complementary PWM (Drives both the top and bottom MOSFETs). 
In AM32,  "Complementary PWM = On" is the same thing as BLHeli32's "non damped Mode" = Off and provides regenerative braking.
With AM32, "Running Brake Level" allows you to config how aggressively the "regenerative braking" occurs (BLheli32 did not provide this type of configurability... I do not know at what setting "Running Brake Level" compares with BLHeli32... more than zero, less than or equal to 10).  
For good melty drifting with AM32, you will want to set "Running Brake Level" very high (possibly max it).

In AM32, "Complementary PWM = On" is the same thing as BLHeli32's "non damped Mode" = Off and provides regenerative braking.

Sorry for the delayed reply... 
We use two batteries in **parallel** for two reasons: (1) as others have said, it does make balancing the weight easier.  This reason was the primary reason until years of experience led me to the second reason. (2) Even for batteries that claim a high "C" rating (supporting high current output), we have found that the batteries, for the equivalent Amp-hours, last longer when setup in parallel than batteries setup in series... Also two batteries in parallel will not see as much voltage-sag as two batteries in series. We run 2x4S in parallel, and could use two larger 2S batteries in series (but I would never recommend that).  We often end our matches with our voltage at 3.8V (very convenient since that is a good storage voltage).

For our larger Melties (12lbs and 30lbs) we do have a separate set of batteries powering the electronics and LEDs (two tiny 2s batteries setup in series) versus the motors are powered by 6S and 8S voltages (once again, two sets of batteries in parallel). In the past (before having a separate battery for the electronics), the power spikes from the motors fried several different BECs and once our electronics (when the BEC goes, the Bot stops).  Since the Melty's brain is also connected to the ESCs (via the signal wire), you need to create a common ground between the two. You could use opto-isolators to completely isolate the two electronic systems, but I have never had any problems with the common ground.

The one downside to this setup is that the batteries wired in parallel form a circuit, and if you leave the batteries connected like that for a long time (days, not hours) the battery's internal cells (the ones with the highest internal resistance) will become drained... causing some other cells (those with the lowest internal resistance) to become over-charged (greater 4.2V).  This destroys the batteries, so you must disconnect the parallel circuit before you head home from the competition (or add an On/Off switch for each battery so they are isolated from each other).

This is very true (and something everyone doing this should worry about and be mindful of)! 
We are always very careful to ensure the batteries are the same voltage when we connect them.  We use the same brand charger, with the same settings to charge the batteries (ideally, we should use the same charger, but that is not always possible).
No matter how careful you are, as soon as your connect two high-discharge LIPOs together in parallel, current will FLOW!
The closer the batteries are to the same voltage, the less the current and the less duration.

With regard to ESC performance referenced in the the_arena_floor-3lb  channel... Some worthwhile things to know:
* We use AM32. I'll post the settings we use below.
* Both AM32 and BLHeli-32 have the ability to break wheel traction when accelerating and decelerating the wheel (when your wheel has poor traction, or gets jarred off the floor).
* When your wheel has excellent traction, the first 5 milliseconds of the pulse window (beginning when you told the motor to go faster or slower), the ESC shows very little response. It is the window of time after that first 4 or 5 milliseconds where the motor starts exerting force between the Bot and the floor. 
* If your rotation speed is very high, then the "pulse window of time" will be shorter.  With AM32 (or BLHeli32), a melty spinning greater than 6000 RPM will not be able to translate due to a "lack of response time" with the motor/ESC system.

Do you have a sense if the 5ms delay is due to software on the esc or due to some inherent physical limitation (transistor switching and magnetic flux both need to be much faster than that to even spin a high kv motor, but there easily could be soemthing I'm forgetting)?

I think it is a 50% physics (it takes time to accelerate mass) and 50% how the ESCs are programmed to behave.

Here is a picture of my "BenchBot"

Those two silver disks are load wheels attached to the same motors we use in our Melty.

They are sized to reflect the amount of inertia that the motors would "feel" for the actual  Melty on the arena floor (assuming great traction and no wheel slippage).

As you can see, I chose two motors that had very similar KV measurements.

Here is a graph of running the motors at 40% power, and then pulsing the motors at different levels of power.

As you can see, the same 40% power curves are not on top of each other.
This is because the battery has a lower voltage, and the RPS of the motors get lower with the batteries lower voltage.

You can also see that the left and right motors, running with the same battery voltage do not overlap due to the motors slightly different KV ratings.

In the above graphs, I show motor RPS without pulsing for one rotation, and then pulse the motors for ten rotations.

In the first rotation or two, you can see the AM32 ESC does a better job decelerating the load wheels, but then the system "recovers" and seems to keeps the average motor RPS somewhat constant.

You can see how, with different positive pulse power values, and negative pulse power values the average motor RPS trends upward or downward.

in general, if you have TRACTION, a bigger delta value will result in a larger impulse to help the Melty drift.
HOWEVER, if your traction is poor, a larger delta value is more likely to create wheel slippage which will make the Melty act like a hockey puck.

Here is a simpler graph to look at...
this one shows load wheel spin-up and slow-down... look at those acceleration and deceleration slopes and "flattening" behaviors (caused by the ESC firmware)

You can see AM32's ability to decelerate the load wheel seems slightly better than its ability to accelerate the load wheel (but it is close).
Also, note the delay between the start at zero and the actual movement of the load wheel (about four or five milliseconds).
Some of that "delay" is the ESC unable to report motor telemetry less than 2.55 RPS due to how the bidirectional dShot telemtry protocol works.

You can also see how the ESC firmware "stops trying very hard" to get to the target power, causing the curves to flatten out as they approach their target.

Yet, I saw the same "slowness" with BLheli32.  

I am not certain... but this is the tool I have, and I have other areas to improve upon before I decide to try to modify or write a better ESC firmware.

A take-away from the above graph... Most three pound Melties should be able to spin-up to full speed within three seconds (if they have good traction).
If they have proper ESC speed control (and good traction), they can do it in two seconds for high speeds and one second for lower speeds.

sorry this is a reply to such an old message, but I'm trying to understand the brake on stop setting and why you suggest not enabling it. I'm confused how it's different from running brake in the context of voltage spikes and heating components. Does it differ in that it doesn't do regenerative braking like running brake does? 

Something that came up recently from <@210442173781508096> was that max braking on his am32 appeared to be shorting the motor phases together and decreasing braking strength would just alternate between coasting and max braking. this would suggest that regen is not happening?

To support re-generative breaking (putting power back into the battery when decelerating), you MUST enable "Complementary PWM" (and your ESC must have six FETs to do complementary PWM).
"Brake on stop" occurs when enabled AND a stop command (dShot cmd=0) or PWM=no throttle. If "Brake on Stop" is not enabled, then AM32 appears to employ the "Running Brake Level".  Previously (like v2.17) it would coast to a stop... taking a long time when inertia/momentum was present; however, now (as you can see in the graph above) it actively performs braking at the set "running brake level" (it will coast to a stop if "running brake level" is set to zero).  I prefer this new behavior.
If "Brake on Stop" is enabled, AM32 will short the motor wires (via the FETs) creating a ton of heat (all your momentum is transformed into heat... VERY BAD THING TO DO WITH A HEAVILY LOADED MOTOR).  "Brake on Stop" is meant for motors (cars and trucks) that want to hold the stopped position. It is intended to be used at low speeds, not going from max speed to stopped.  If you must use it in your Melty, have your software set a low speed (for a while) before transitioning to stopped.
"Running Brake Level" enables you to specify a less steep slope when decreasing motor speeds.  In the above "spin-up" and spin-down graph, you can see the slope for accelerating and decelerating a load wheel that represents my Melty's motor load.  In that test,  I had "Running Brake Level" set to 10 (the max value).  I can decrease the deceleration slope by decreasing the "Running Brake Level" value, but I cannot make it any steeper (except by using "brake on stop", which would lock the wheel, abrade my wheels, and possibly burn out my motor).

One cool thing about Melty's and "Complementary PWM" is that when you decelerate one motor while accelerating the other motor, the "recovered power" of the decelerating motor is being transferred to the accelerating motor. **<- I think this is why you see a "stabilizing" behavior after one or two pulsing cycles.**

Here is what I check for every loop:
(1)  Is there new RC control data from the RC receiver (this is handled asynchronously via the UART, the library uses interrupts as data is received); transmitter is sending a full RC controller packet (16 channels) at 111Hz.
(2a) Updating the two ESCs at 8000Hz; the logic checks:  (a) where in the rotation is the melty right now? (b) Do I want to change my motor speeds?  This is implemented using the Teensy 4.0's SPI peripheral, one for each motor. It does use two interrupts (data sent, data received), but the peripheral manages all the data buffering. (2b) Reading the wheel eRPM from the ESCs (bidirectional dShot), then making decisions based on that data (8000Hz).
(3)  Reading the two accelerometers at 1000Hz.   How fast are we spinning?  Making decisions based on this data (rate of rotation). 
(4) Blinking the LEDs.  Is it time to turn an LED on or off.

Yes, "sinusoidal control" is the objective, but how are you supposed to do that?   This is the primary secret sauce ingredient for each of our melties. The ESCs do not instantly drive the motor at the speed you desire; there are: ESC/Motor latency, Bot/Motor/wheel inertia, traction issues, and ESC control limitations.  Those factors all confound "sinusoidal control".  Back to what drove your question... if I start and end my "sinusoidal control" 10% to 20% late (or early) each half rotation, that will have a negative impact on how well my melty translates. Therefore, being able to update the motor speeds at the exact moment in the rotation you desire is important (requiring a very high ESC command update rate).  Having "synchronous delays" (due to a slow synchronous I2C or SPI implementation) will degrade the mely's performance.

Hence the interest in my loop times histogram, which impacted motor update rates.

FYI... posted this on the help channel:
There are two (similar, but different) dShot implementations: (1) dShot, and (2) bidirectional dShot (with eRPS telemetry and optional extended telemetry: current, voltage, and temp).
Years ago, I implemented both on the Teensy 4.0 using the SPI peripheral (both dShot and bidirectional dShot), and the PWM peripherals (just dShot).
My code for my bidirectional dShot implementation  was posted to the Teensy forum at:  https://forum.pjrc.com/index.php?threads/implementing-bidirectional-dshot-on-teensy-3-2.62635/#post-268116
The code snippets found at the above forum are 98% of the working code; I never turned this into a library.  Working on a low-level protocol like this requires a scope so you can see the timing; without that low level timing data, it is very difficult to debug.
It could be much simpler to develop this using the UART peripheral; with the one-wire bidirectional dShot being connected to both the Tx and Rx, and some clever programming (I'm just not sure if the UARTs are fast enough for dShot600 or dShot1200, but dShot300 ot dShot150 should be possible).  As you indicated, it comes down to how well you can construct the correct 37.5% and 75% duty cycles with the peripheral of your choice.  Most ESCs are just looking for that ratio of ON/OFF, and the exact speed (150/300/600/1200) is not as important. 
The above code is not portable to other MCUs... the code contains a large number of low level register writes.

I am not sure what caused that lock-up... I think the processor went into its physical software load state.  However, I completely removed that button from the board.

I can think of two possible explanations:
(1) I have some infinite loop hidden somewhere in my code.
(2) The Teensy processor entered its "programming mode":  A physical pushbutton is provided to allow recovery from bad code. Pressing the button button puts Teensy into programming mode. It is not a "reset button" which restarts your program. The button is dedicated to recovery from bad code. A Program pin also allows external hardware to force entry to programming mode.

Regarding #2, even though I removed the button, I left the pin pad exposed (just in case I needed it).  My current theory is that if a loose (plastic/silicone shielded) wire with a static voltage touched that pin and pulled it to a ground state, then the Teensy would enter programming mode.

I will try to reproduce the behavior on the bench.

On my beetle melty with an ESP8266 module, it was having weird issues where the baud rate would switch to 74880 (the default for programming). It was like it was being put into a semi boot mode, as the code would still run. 
I mostly resolved it by adding some ceramic capacitors to the relevant I/O pins that set boot mode. I think static buildup was coming in from the heading LED pin.
